title: Space Tech Briefing — July 01, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space enthusiasts and welcome to your daily dose of the cosmos! It is July 1st, 2026, and we are absolutely buzzing with the latest from across the galaxy and right here on Earth. Let's dive in!

First up, for all you rocketeers and future astronauts, get ready for some high-flying action! The C’Space event has officially opened its doors in Ger, France, today! This is your chance to witness experimental rockets taking flight. Imagine the roar, the spectacle! And for the younger generation, or even the young at heart, there's a fantastic water rocket workshop. It’s hands-on fun that truly brings the thrill of space exploration to life. Don't miss it!

And speaking of summer fun, the French Space Agency, CNES, has just rolled out an incredible lineup to keep your minds soaring all season long. They're inviting you to explore the cosmos right from your deckchair! Think immersive videos, captivating podcasts, and fascinating astronomical readings, perfect for solo discovery or family adventures. Get ready for a summer rich in spatial discoveries. It’s the perfect way to turn those lazy days into cosmic journeys.

Now, as we await today's new launches, let's take a moment to look back at a major recent development that's still creating interstellar ripples. Just last week, on June 24th, the European Space Agency unveiled a truly monumental image from their Euclid space telescope. We are talking about the largest, highest-resolution photo ever taken in visible light of the very heart of our Milky Way! This colossal 6-gigapixel mosaic reveals over 60 million stars in a single scene. It offers unprecedented detail of our galaxy’s incredibly dense central region. Euclid captured this stunning view back in March 2025, during a 26-hour observation run, pointing its camera towards the galactic bulge and stitching together nine distinct shots. Each one covered a portion of the sky larger than the full Moon! To ensure we saw the true colors, data from the ground-based Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope was blended with Euclid's visible light information. Absolutely breathtaking!

And that’s your morning briefing, packed with the latest from our incredible universe. Thank you for joining us, and remember, the sky is never the limit! Have a stellar day.
